data.title: Monster Beverage: Premium Valuation, But 2027 Upside Remains
data.description:
This year, I have expanded my coverage to the beverage industry. I already covered The Coca-Cola Company (KO) and PepsiCo (PEP), and in this report, I am adding Monster Beverage Corporation (MNST) to my coverage. I believe the company has some appeal following the recent share price decline that makes it a buy, but there are some risks as well.

Monster: Less Reliant on Emerging Market and Out-of-Home Sales but With Risks

In my report on The Coca-Cola Company, I already noted that while people will not stop drinking beverages when the economy cools, beverage companies are not insulated from slowing GDP growth. That also somewhat explains why the stock prices of beverage companies have come under pressure since the war in Iran started. For that reason, it is also not a major surprise that we see a company such as Monster trade around 12% lower compared to its pre-war highs.

There are some elements, however, that may make Monster Beverage attractive. The lower share price for a long-term investment may provide an attractive entry point. However, there is more to it than just a lower share price. When economic growth cools, we generally see out-of-home channels cool a bit faster, and Monster is a bit less focused on out-of-home compared to, for instance, Coca-Cola. This could make the stock somewhat more attractive. At the same time, we note that Monster is less reliant on emerging markets, which tend to be sensitive to GDP growth disruptions. Monster derives more of its sales from North America and Europe.

So, beyond my coverage expansion in the beverage industry, Monster Beverage caught my attention for a potential investment for the following reasons:

  1. Lower share price compared to pre-war setting.
  2. Less reliant on out-of-home sales channels.
  3. Less reliance on GDP-sensitive emerging markets.

That, however, does not mean that this makes Monster a buy for the simple reason that there are also risks such as increasing aluminum prices and forex rates along with an overarching demand risk.

Monster Has a Poor Track Record of Satisfying Wall Street Estimates

While it is definitely the case that estimates may soften in the weeks and months ahead, we note that revenue estimates have held up well, with $2.15 billion in sales expected, signaling growth of 16.2%. Over the past month, the downward revision was 0.14%, with +2.4% and +4.2% for the three- and six-month periods. So, those are largely positive revisions for the upcoming quarter. We note that for Q2 and Q3 there has been a 1.1% upward revision. So, we are not seeing major negative revisions at this point.

On earnings per share, 16.5% growth is expected to $0.52, with estimates improving 1.8% over the past six months but down over the past 3 months. We also note that for Q2 and Q3, EPS estimates have declined by 2.8% and 0.5%, but that still signals double-digit year-on-year growth.

The problematic part for Monster is that its track record of beating analyst estimates is not great. On revenues and EPS, the company has missed estimates 7 out of the last 12 quarters, with the slight positive being that the company beat estimates over the last three quarters. With demand and cost risk increasing, the track record of Monster is not really a promising one.

Monster Beverage Initiated With Buy Rating

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

Monster Beverage trades at a significant premium compared to the peer group, but that is something that I have seen with companies such as Coca-Cola and Pepsi as well. So, I am not too concerned about the lack of upside against a peer group valuation. What we do see is that the stock price currently does not have the 2026 earnings priced in, while typically at this point we would see that 2026 earnings should be priced in fully. So, that adds some appeal, and against expected earnings for 2027, we see 20% upside with a $92.28 price target.

Expected annual sale growth through 2028 is expected to be around 9% with nearly 11% annual growth in EBITDA and 8.8% growth in free cash flow. The free cash flow generation is not exactly what I would like to see since it falls short of EBITDA and sales growth. We also note the lack of a dividend and potential higher cost and demand risk. However, the company also has no bank debt, and that is really strong as it potentially allows the company to repurchase shares when share prices fall.

Margins are expected to continue rising in the years ahead, but again we note the potential for higher costs. The free cash flow generation is expected to be modest this year, and that also drives the apparent low free cash flow growth against sales and EBITDA growth. We note, however, that this is driven by lower capital expenditures last year, with an increase expected this year. I have not modeled in any share repurchases, but we note that, given the company’s net cash position, there is space to repurchase shares, and especially if share prices weaken further, the appeal to leverage the cash position to repurchase shares grows rapidly.

Conclusion: No Dividend but I Like Monster

While there definitely are risks and the free cash flow growth this year does not look strong compared to last year, I do believe that Monster has appeal even without dividends. The reason is that the stock is trading at a discount to its expected earnings this year, and its net cash position does allow the company to buy back shares, and the impact that makes will grow substantially when share prices are depressed. So, while there are some clear risks, I do believe that the shares are a buy, and if share prices contract further to a level below a trading level of 25.8x EV/EBITDA, it would even be a strong buy.

data.title: KeyCorp: Likely Fairly Valued
data.description:
With the help of record-breaking profits in the bank's fee-generating businesses, KeyCorp (KEY) managed to easily exceed analyst expectations for its bottom and top line in the first fiscal quarter. KeyCorp also benefited from an expansion in its loan portfolio and higher net interest income which were driven especially by growth in its commercial loans. While the bank’s deposits dipped slightly on a sequential basis, the bank saw strong net interest income growth in Q1’26, fueled by favorable deposit mix changes, and the NII outlook is positive as well in the short term. KeyCorp, however, is trading above its longer term P/B ratio and shares appear to be fully valued now, in my opinion.

Previous rating

I rated shares of KeyCorp a hold in my last coverage in January, Strong NII, $1.2B Stock Buyback, Not Cheap, because the regional banking enterprise benefited from higher-for-longer interest rates that allowed the lender to grow its net interest income at double-digits. The bank also guided for more than $1.0B in stock buybacks at the time, providing support for its shares.

Offsetting these positive trends in earnings and capital returns were expectations of falling interest rates in 2026. In the fourth-quarter, KeyCorp continued to see strong performance in its core lending business, but KeyCorp trails regional banking rivals in terms of ROE performance. I believe shares have limited upside here in 2026 and I confirm my hold rating following the first-quarter earnings scorecard last week.

NII and fee businesses drive earnings growth

In Q1’26, the regional banking platform reported $0.44 per-share in non-GAAP earnings, beating the consensus estimate by $0.03 per-share. The revenue figure came in at $1.95B, $14.5M above the analyst consensus due to strong growth for both the bank's net interest income as well as capital markets-oriented fee businesses.

The first-quarter was a robust quarter for banks in general, including KeyCorp, as the Federal Reserve took a cautious approach to rate cuts and U.S. economic growth remains strong. While a new war in the Middle East and turmoil surrounding the Strait of Hormuz, a vital oil artery, have weighed on investor sentiment in early March, the lending market overall is in great shape as well.

KeyCorp benefited from robust loan growth momentum, especially in the commercial segment, driving earnings out-performance vs. Wall Street estimates. For the most recent quarter, KeyCorp reported $107.7B in average loans, showing 3.2% year-over-year growth.

The key to the bank's expansion of its loan base in the first-quarter was strong demand for loans from the commercial sector in a resilient economy: KeyCorp's average commercial loans were published at $78.1B, showing 7.9% year-over-year growth. In other words, the bank's commercial loan activity – which focuses primarily on middle market business and specialized industry sectors – was the main engine behind KeyCorp's loan growth in Q1'26.

In terms of net interest income – a core figure for banks that engage in large-scale loan originations – KeyCorp benefited from favorable changes in its deposit mix as well as fixed asset repricing, resulting in an 11% year-over-year increase in its net interest income: for the first fiscal quarter, the lender reported $1.23B in net interest income while its net interest margin expanded 0.29 PP year-over-year.

KeyCorp expects to generate approximately 9% to 10% year-over-year growth in its net interest income (previously 8-10%) which puts the expected NII range up to around $5.1B for FY 2026. This growth will be driven chiefly by expectations of a higher-for-longer federal fund rate environment as well as tailwinds for income from an expanded loan portfolio.

The war in the Middle East has made it more likely that the Federal Reserve will postpone Fed Fund rate cuts in the near term. In March, the Fed maintained stable federal fund rates in a range of 3.50-3.75% which was driven by concerns about accelerating inflation. Last month, inflation indeed soared to 3.3%, the highest level in two years. However, while banks may benefit from a higher-for-longer rate environment short term, in the longer term interest rates are most certainly going to fall... which poses earnings risks for banks in general.

Further, KeyCorp benefited from robust momentum in its fee businesses, especially investment banking and related services. The bank reported total fee income of $723M, showing 8% year-over-year growth.

While KeyCorp’s fee income did not grow as quickly as its net interest income, a more differentiated view shows that investment banking (M&A advisory and debt-related capital markets advisory) as well as trust & investment services were key to the bank’s growth in Q1’26, with both segments expanding their earnings by 13% year-over-year.

Both segments are dependent on strong equity market performance and an appetite for corporations to raise capital, so a strong U.S. economy is key to KeyCorp’s fee income strength for the remainder of 2026.

KeyCorp’s valuation

KeyCorp has seen, like so many other banks, a major revaluation to the upside in the months of March and April... which was driven by subsiding fears about a prolonged conflict between the U.S. and Iran in the Middle East.

Shares of the regional lender are currently trading at a price-to-book ratio of 1.36X which compares against higher P/B ratios for U.S. Bancorp (USB) and Fifth Third Bancorp (FITB): these banks have price-to-book ratios of 1.50X and 1.43X, with the industry group average price-to-book ratio sitting at 1.42X.

In my last review of KeyCorp I calculated a fair value of $18.71 per-share, using the firm's historical P/B ratio of 1.15X at the time. The regional bank's book value as of the end of the first-quarter was $16.13 (+8.3% Y/Y, -0.9% Q/Q), so the application of the same valuation factor leads me to an updated post-Q1 fair value of $18.55 per-share.

While my fair value estimate for KEY is lower than the bank's share price, I am maintaining a hold rating for the regional lender due to uncertainty about the Fed's exact rate cut timeline, the existence of price-supporting stock buybacks (raised to $1.3B in Q1'26, from $1.2B) as well as the underlying strength of the U.S. economy which props up KeyCorp's capital markets business.

Risks with KeyCorp

KeyCorp’s biggest risk is a faster-than-expected timeline for the Federal Reserve to lower federal fund rates which could happen in the context of a deescalating conflict in the Middle East. Rising crude oil prices contributed to the Federal Reserve delaying rate cuts in March. A rapid drop in energy prices, potentially catalyzed by a peace agreement between the U.S. and Iran could potentially accelerate the Fed’s rate cut timeline, and add to growing earnings and NII pressure for the regional bank going forward.

Final thoughts

KeyCorp reported better-than-expected earnings and revenues for its first fiscal quarter, exceeding analyst estimates, due to strong net interest income and fee income tailwinds in its business, especially in investment banking and advisory. While the U.S. economy is performing well right now, despite higher inflation, I see limited upside for KeyCorp in 2026 given that its shares are already trading approximately 21% above the bank's 3-year average P/B ratio. Most importantly, I have reduced my exposure to the banking sector in general in expectation of falling benchmark rates in 2026 which indicates the potential for contracting net interest income margins. As a result, I only maintain a hold rating for KeyCorp, mainly due to the bank's stock buybacks and 4% dividend yield.

Your Premium trial has ended

data.title: USFR: Ultrashort-Dated Floating-Rate Treasuries For Liquidity
data.description:
The WisdomTree Floating Rate Treasury Fund ETF (USFR) is a fixed-income exchange-traded fund designed to provide investors with exposure to short-dated, floating-rate US Treasuries. With the short time to maturity and floating rate feature of the fund, investors will be fully exposed to Federal Reserve interest rate policy and the market’s expectations towards rate cuts or hikes. With uncertainty growing in the markets regarding the Federal Reserve’s rate policy for 2026 following a dramatic rise in oil prices, USFR can be a candidate for investors who believe that the Fed will hike rates in their attempt to fend off inflationary pressures, though may face a decline if the rate-cutting cycle were to continue. Ideally, investors may seek to utilize USFR when the key rate is expected to be hiked while using a fixed-rate strategy for rate cuts.

About WisdomTree Floating Rate Treasury Fund ETF

USFR was launched on February 4, 2014 on the NYSE Arca Exchange. The ETF has a net expense ratio of 15bps, aligned with peer short-term Treasury strategies on the market. USFR exhibits substantial liquidity with $17.17 billion in assets with an average of $260 million in share value changing hands on a daily basis at the time of writing.

The ETF pays out a monthly distribution based on the auction spread and interest income from the floating-rate notes. The distribution over the last 12 months summed to $2.02/share, yielding 4%. Given that the strategy holds FRNs, distributions can vary drastically from year to year depending on the Federal Reserve’s interest rate policy and the economic cycle. During zero interest rate policy [ZIRP], the distribution was effectively cut given the yield curve tapering as held by the Fed at the time.

Resulting from the rate hike cycle in recent years, the distribution returned, peaking in 2023/2024, before falling back in 2025 following the end of the rate-hiking cycle and the beginning of the rate-cutting cycle.

Essentially, USFR will nearly fully reflect the Fed’s interest rate policy as the “floating” aspect of the interest rate will adjust in accordance with the target range. In terms of the auction spread for new issuances, the spread tends to widen during a period of interest rate cuts while tightening during interest rate hikes. The rationale behind this is that FRNs become more valuable if interest rates are hiked due to the adjusted interest rate tied to the notes. As opposed to fixed-rate notes, which exhibit an inverse correlation to interest rate policy, floating-rate notes exhibit more positive correlation as a result of the appreciating value of the coupon during rate hikes.

Factors to Consider Before Investing in USFR

USFR can be considered as a macro strategy in which investors will base their investment decision on the direction of the Fed’s interest rate policy. Factors worth considering include inflation, GDP growth, and employment. Interest rate policy is the first tool in the Fed’s arsenal deployed to fend from heightened inflationary pressures, as exhibited throughout 2022-2023. When the market virtually collapsed in 2020 due to liquidity lockup and a halt in economic activity, the Fed cut the key rate to ZIRP, providing easy financing to companies in need.

Following the pandemic lockdowns, inflation became a challenge, resulting in drastic rate increases in 50bps increments. This effectively tightened borrowing costs in an attempt to push down the rate of inflation.

2026 started with optimism regarding the moderating rate of inflation before the war of Iran hit the oil markets, sending oil prices as high as $112/bbl before falling back on early signs of a peace agreement. As a result of higher oil prices, headline inflation jumped in for the month of March 2026 after multiple months of sequential decline. As a result of this increase in inflation, the Fed has been on the sidelines, looking for an indication as to the direction of the market and how things will play out. With oil futures coming off their highs, the market may be correcting to a more normalized environment.

One useful tool for investors to monitor is the FedWatch tool offered by CME Group, Inc. (CME). The tool can be utilized to evaluate the market’s expectation of interest rate policy changes by month. While this shouldn’t be used to forecast interest rate policy, it can help the investor understand the pricing mechanisms for FRNs and help understand how the market perceives future rate policy. For example, there is a 66% probability that rates will remain unchanged through September 2026 and an emerging change that the rate may be cut by 25bps.

Investor Suitability

USFR can be used by a variety of investors seeking liquidity and exposure to short-dated FRNs. The strategy can be used for liquidity given its high trading volumes and depth. USFR can be used to park unallocated funds or as a component of an investor’s fixed income strategy. USFR can be best utilized during rate-hiking cycles given the adjusted coupon rate on the FRNs.

Risks Related to USFR

USFR directly exposes investors’ interest rate policy set by the Federal Reserve, meaning that distributions may adjust up or down when interest rates are hiked or cut. This can, in turn, influence cash flows received while holding this strategy throughout the rate cycle. USFR exhibits next to no credit risk with notes backed by the full faith of the US Government. USFR has an effective duration of 0.02 years, meaning that the price of the notes will exhibit minimal pricing adjustments relating to Fed interest rate policy.

Final Thoughts

USFR can be used by investors seeking exposure to short-dated floating-rate US Treasury notes. USFR can be used for liquidity given its robust distribution yield and low cost. Investors must consider interest rate policy before investing in this strategy as future cash flows may greatly fluctuate throughout the rate cycle.

This article answers three questions about USFR:

  1. What kind of holdings does USFR have?
  2. What affects USFR's price and yield?
  3. How can USFR be used in a portfolio?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: Kimberly-Clark: This 5% Yielder Is A Buy Ahead Of The Kenvue Merger
data.description:
I haven't covered consumer products company Kimberly-Clark (NYSE:KMB) since late 2024. At the time, I argued that shares were a hold, as the company's fine valuation was offset by limited growth prospects.

With the benefit of hindsight, the valuation wasn't low enough to make for a good entry point. In the back half of 2025, Kimberly-Clark stumbled out of the bottom of its five-year trading range, and shares further slipped to below the $100/share level to start the current year:

The drop came about, in large part, due to negative market reaction around Kimberly-Clark's planned mega-merger with Kenvue (KVUE).

While that negative reaction is understandable, if successful, this merger could solve the underlying growth issue that has plagued Kimberly-Clark in recent years. In any case, the combination of the pending merger and lower starting valuation have done enough to warrant an upgrade for KMB stock today.

On The Kenvue Deal

I had a bullish position in Kenvue stock prior to Kimberly-Clark announcing its M&A deal last fall. The takeaway from that being that I felt Kenvue had fallen significantly below fair value and marked an attractive opportunity at its pre-deal trading price. In my view, that discount came about because of an overly negative assessment of Kenvue's liability risk tied to Tylenol and its purported links to autism. While there is certainly the possibility of negative legal outcomes, the scientific evidence does not appear to be especially worrisome for Tylenol and thus my assumption is that the ultimate financial cost tied to Tylenol liabilities will be manageable.

I see a lot of commentary on the deal suggesting that Kenvue is in an operational rut. Purely if you go off the stock price, I can see how people come to that conclusion. However, if you put the legal matters aside, I believe KVUE stock would otherwise still be trading in the $20s and there would be more balanced sentiment around the stock.

Look at Kenvue's Q4 2025 results, for example. EPS of 27 cents beat by five cents and rose by a penny versus the prior year. Revenues of $3.8 billion grew by 3.3% and exceeded expectations by a healthy amount. This isn't explosive growth by any means. But it's quite fine given the challenging macroeconomic landscape which consumer staples firms have been facing over the past year.

In my view, the main issue with Kenvue is not its operational results, but rather the uncertain but potentially large legal overhang. Kimberly-Clark is getting a good value in purchasing Kenvue because it is willing to absorb that legal risk which other firms were shying away from. Only time will tell how the legal issues ultimately resolve, but from an operating business standpoint, I expect a combined Kimberly-Clark/Kenvue to surprise analysts to the upside.

A Favorable Post-Merger Outlook

Specifically, the combined firm would be the world's No. 2 player in consumer health and wellness, trailing only P&G (PG). I believe this improved size would give Kimberly-Clark improved bargaining power with both suppliers and retailers. This, in turn, should help Kimberly-Clark reverse recent shelf space losses with key retail clients.

In addition, a firm of this size would have significant efficiency opportunities; management is looking for $2 billion in cost synergies which would work out to roughly 15% of Kenvue's overall cost of goods sold. Kenvue is already selling at 15x this year's estimated earnings. If the cost savings come through along the lines that Kimberly-Clark expects, the company is getting an excellent deal that can power the next few years of the firm's EPS and dividend growth.

Furthermore, I'd point out that Kimberly-Clark has still kept its EPS pointing generally upward over the past 20 years despite its challenges in growing top-line revenues. While this is hardly explosive growth, it has been significant progress over the years:

In addition to cost-cutting and operational work to boost margins, Kimberly-Clark has also managed this continuing EPS growth due to a relentless buyback program. The company had about 500 million shares of stock outstanding 20 years ago, with that dropping to about 330 million shares today. That's a huge cumulative reduction. A benefit of having a relatively cheap and unloved stock is that the buyback can retire shares at an aggressive clip.

Of course, the share count will go back up once stock is issued in consideration for the Kenvue purchase. However, that should be at least equally offset by the new earnings power that comes on board with Kenvue's assets.

In the bigger picture, Kimberly-Clark is a Dividend King with an astounding 53 years of consecutive dividend increases. As the company's EPS continues to grow, and should get a substantial boost with the Kenvue purchase, the conditions are there for the dividend streak to continue for years to come.

KMB Stock Verdict

Kimberly-Clark first started to trade for around $100 per share way back in the year 2013. In other words, it's been 13 lost years for the company as far as share price appreciation goes.

However, the company has improved its profitability and in particular its EPS over the years. Admittedly, it's been at a slow rate. But EPS has advanced from around $5/share in the mid-2010s to about $7.50 recently. There's enough underlying growth to drive a moderately higher stock price. And now, with the current pessimism around the stock, the yield has advanced to 5.1%.

Aside from a momentary blip during the 2008 financial crisis, this is the only time over the past 25 years that KMB stock has yielded this much:

For dividend growth investors, this looks like an opportunity. Some people might say that the Kenvue merger could give the company a chance to reset the dividend policy and put through an effective cut to the payout going forward. I'll grant that this is possible.

However, Kenvue is similarly profitable to Kimberly-Clark, and Kenvue shares yield 4.7% at the moment as well. Thus, this is a merger of two solidly profitable firms with above-average yields. There's no inherent reason why the combined entity would need to deviate from the present course in terms of shareholder yield.

The one caveat to my buy rating for Kimberly-Clark is that there's a decent case for buying Kenvue rather than KMB stock today. That's because of the ongoing merger premium. Kimberly-Clark is offering $3.50 per share in cash plus 0.14625 shares of KMB stock for each outstanding share of Kenvue. Based on Friday's close, Kimberly-Clark is trading at $98.84. This implies that the equity portion of the deal would be worth $14.46 and then there is the $3.50 cash kicker on top off that. This would work out to $17.96 if the deal closed today per share of Kenvue. Kenvue closed at $17.61 on Friday, so there's roughly a 2% discount in buying KVUE here with the intention of it converting into KMB stock, rather than buying Kimberly-Clark outright. That's not a big difference at the end of the day, but a discount is a discount.

Be it via Kenvue or with Kimberly-Clark stock directly, I'm at a buy rating on this consumer staples entity ahead of its merger. Kimberly-Clark has a long and successful track record as a consumer products company. Recent years haven't been as kind to the firm, but we shouldn't lose sight of the fact that it has continued notching record EPS figures despite the stagnation on the top line. And, with the attractively priced Kenvue deal likely set to close later this year, that should set the stage for the company's next stage of shareholder value creation.

In addition, with the current unsettled economic outlook, I believe Kimberly-Clark's 5.1% dividend yield and long history of annual dividend increases will stand out as an attractive option for income investors.

data.title: IPG Photonics: Stock Could Outperform On Above-Average Long-Term Growth
data.description:
I'm revisiting a company that I wrote about over a decade ago in the fiber laser space. IPG Photonics (IPGP) has strong expected above-average growth with a reasonable valuation. Margins have been expanding, which has been driving strong earnings growth. IPGP is trading in a bull flag formation which could lead to a breakout for the stock. Refer to the link above for more detailed company background information.

Growth Catalysts

IPG Photonics returned to a positive growth year in terms of revenue in 2025. Revenue increased about 3% yoy in 2025. This was followed by three years in a row of revenue declines from 2022 to 2024. IPGP achieved strong growth for its welding products in Asia as battery investments rebounded in China. There is a demand shift away from electric vehicles to storage batteries. Storage batteries require more sophisticated welding methods. IPGP is also experiencing higher demand for their solutions in battery manufacturing or consumer and medical devices.

The company's medical sales increased 21% yoy in 2025. IPG Photonics gained a new major customer which contributed to that increase. IPGP also received FDA clearance for its next-generation urology system. The clearance was for IPG's proprietary StoneSense and advanced modulation technologies. This solution allows surgeons to tell the difference between soft tissue and kidney stones. This improves precision and control during these procedures. StoneSense began shipping during Q4 2025.

IPG also developed CROSSBOW, the company's 1st stand-alone system for defense uses. CROSSBOW is a cost-effective, scalable laser defense system that neutralizes threats from small group 1 & 2 drones. As part of this initiative, IPG started its IPG Defense division to focus on product development and customer engagement. The IPG Defense initiative includes a new manufacturing plant with an office. This could be another growth market for IPGP.

The book-to-bill ratio of 1 from Q4 2025 indicates improving market conditions with increased customer demand. The company is seeing progress with improvements in medical, advanced applications, and micromachining. IPG expects momentum in these areas to continue in 2026.

IPG Photonics has been streamlining operations and increasing product development. The efforts to streamline operations led to margin expansion in 2025. The gross margin increased from 34.6% to 38% from 2024 to 2025. The operating margin increased from 1.5% to 2.3%, while the net income margin increased from a negative (18.6%) to a positive 3% over the same time. These margin gains should help drive strong earnings growth going forward.

The company has been strengthening its position in industrial applications. This includes accelerating the use of lasers in large markets and growing via new business. IPGP's solutions are aiming to replace older technology. IPGP's offerings help customers address complex issues where efficiency and precision are the priorities.

IPG's laser cleaning solutions are an example of how the company is replacing incumbent technology such as less desirable chemical and abrasive methods. This offering comes from IPGP's acquisition of cleanLASER which completed in December 2024. The cleanLASER offering provides customers with safe, effective, and environmentally-friendly solutions. This can be more desirable than chemical-based cleaning methods.

At the same time, IPG is entering new nonindustrial markets where IPG's laser solutions provide cost benefits and better outcomes than previous methods. These strategies should help IPG Photonics to expand its laser applications in high growth areas such as micromachining, medical, and directed energy. This will help drive the company's long-term growth.

Balance Sheet

IPG Photonics has a strong balance sheet. The company has $839 million in total cash with just $17.3 million in total debt, leaving no net debt. IPG has 6x more current assets than current liabilities and over 8x more total assets than total liabilities for total equity of $2.1 billion. This strong balance sheet puts IPGP in a great position to grow the business while maintaining the ability to repurchase stock. IPG repurchased $54.2 million worth of stock in 2025.

Valuation

The PEG ratio seems to be the best valuation metric to use for IPGP. The reason for that is due to the company's strong expected multiple-year earnings growth. IPGP is currently trading with a PEG of 1.56. This is lower than the sector median PEG of 1.45. IPGP's PEG is based on its estimated 3 to 5 years annual earnings growth of 45%.

Competitors, Coherent (COHR) and Lumentum (LITE) are trading with PEG ratios of 1.69 and 0.87 respectively. IPGP is trading in the middle of these peers. Despite the low PEG ratio, Lumentum looks very overbought from a technical stock perspective right now with a bearish divergence on the weekly chart. Bearish divergences typically result in sharp corrections from what I have observed. Coherent is not yet showing a bearish divergence, but the stock is overbought with the RSI above 80 on the weekly chart. So, I see IPGP positioned better for near-term upside from a technical standpoint.

Technical Perspective

IPG's weekly chart above displays a bull flag formation that formed in 2026. Bull flag formations typically result in the stock breaking out above the peak of the flagpole. This would be above $156.

The RSI remains bullish as it remains above the 50 level. The RSI has plenty of room to move higher since it remains below the oversold level of 70. The stock did move into overbought territory back in February before it pulled back.

The MACD indicator remains bullish as the blue line remains above the red signal line while the histogram bars remain green. The histogram bars did decrease in length. But that could just reflect the recent pullback as part of the flag portion of the bull flag formation. The flag is a consolidation period that tends to occur on profit-taking after the strong run-up in price (flagpole).

The next earnings call for Q1 2026 results, scheduled for May 5, 2026, could be the next catalyst for the stock. IPGP is estimating to achieve adjusted EBITDA of $25 to $40 million and adjusted diluted EPS of $0.10 to $0.40. Those are wide ranges. So, actual results in the upper range or above those ranges could lead to a rally for the stock.

The Risks for Stock

Downturns in the markets that IPG Photonics serves could lead to revenue and earnings declines. IPGP serves markets such as materials processing, aerospace, automotive, photovoltaic, manufacturing, heavy industry, etc. These markets can be cyclical. The materials processing market comprised 86% of IPG's total revenue in 2025. So, IPG could experience significant rough patches during slowdowns in these markets.

IPGP obtains a high concentration of sales from its customers in China. These customers comprised 29% of total sales in 2025. Unfavorable conditions for these customers and/or unfavorable conditions related to China would have a likely negative impact on IPG's revenue. These conditions can include: economic downturns, effects of tariffs, costly laws/regulations, etc.

The laser industry is highly competitive. IPGP competes largely on price and technology. Some of IPG's competitors are larger with more resources. Increased competition could take market share away from IPG Photonics and/or make conditions more difficult to grow. Increased competition can result in lower prices, which leads to less revenue. The industry has experienced lower prices due to this high competition.

IPG Photonics Long-Term Outlook

IPG Photonics appears to be on the right track for long-term growth. The company has been targeting growth markets with its attractive solutions. IPG's efforts to streamline the business have been driving strong margin expansion. So, the company has solid strategies to grow the top and bottom lines.

IPG's above-average long-term earnings growth has the potential to drive long-term outperforming gains for the stock. The reasonable valuation in terms of the PEG ratio can support strong stock growth.

data.title: Union Bankshares: Small, But Steady
data.description:
In this column, we shift our regional bank coverage to northern New England with the just-reported Q1 2026 earnings from Union Bankshares, Inc. (UNB). Based in Morrisville, Vermont, Union Bank serves a distinct geographic niche across Vermont and New Hampshire. Much like our other analyses this earnings season, we find this earnings investigations offer us a unique look at local economies. They can tell us if we are starting to see signs of economic stress. While we are very early in this earnings season, so far we have not seen any cause for concern. Lending activity remains healthy and borrowers are paying. Looking into Union and its service area, this region relies on a mix of local resilience and seasonal tourism. Early indications from this quarter suggest that while the New England economy isn't sprinting, it remains on remarkably stable footing.

This bank, which we continue to rate a Hold, just reported its results. While the bank is much smaller than some of the other names in our coverage, its performance provides critical data points for the evidence base we use to track the health of the consumer. Let us discuss the metrics.

Union Bankshares Q1 Revenue and Net Interest Income

Union Bankshares reported a pretty strong Q1, with consolidated net income rising to $3.0 million, or $0.65 per share. Folks, this was a healthy jump from the $0.55 we saw in the same period last year and an improvement over the $0.60 reported in the sequential Q4.

The big driver here was net interest income, which improved by $1.0 million, or 10.0%, compared to a year ago. Total interest income hit $19.5 million, driven by a larger earning asset base and the higher-rate environment. However, we did see some increases on the expense side. Non-interest expenses climbed nearly 10% to $10.8 million, largely due to rising salaries and employee benefits. This is a common theme in our 2026 coverage so far, banks are making more on their spreads, but labor and other costs are keeping a bit of a lid on the full earnings potential.

Union Bankshares Loans and Deposits

We always watch trends in lending as a proxy for local economic strength. In Q1, loan growth was slow, but steady. Total loans ended the quarter at $1.18 billion, a modest 1.3% increase over the prior year. This suggests that while there isn't a massive surge in new credit demand in the region, borrowers aren't retreating either. On the deposit front, the bank ended the quarter with $1.20 billion in total deposits. It is worth noting deposits have actually grown slightly year-over-year from a baseline of $1.18 billion, in the initial release there was a typo of $1.81 billion, which would have been an eyepopping loss of deposits if true. The bank continues to utilize some brokered deposits ($31.8 million) to manage liquidity, but the core customer base remains the primary driver.

A Surprising Boost in Asset Quality

The most notable part of this report was the credit loss recovery. While many regional banks are still tucking money away for potential rainy days as loan loss provisions, Union Bank recorded a credit loss benefit of $325,000 this quarter, compared to an expense of $235,000 a year ago. So this swing suggests that the bank’s existing reserves are more than sufficient and that their current loan book is performing exceptionally well. The allowance for credit losses actually dipped slightly to $8.07 million. As we see it, this is a clear sign that, at least in this pocket of New England, borrowers are paying back their loans with very little issue. Asset quality remains a major strength for this name.

Return Metrics and Outlook

Profitability metrics for UNB remain quite impressive for a bank of this size. The return on equity stands at 15% for the last twelve months, and the book value per share rose a significant 13.1% year-over-year to $17.46. We also want to point out that management remains committed to returning capital, maintaining its quarterly dividend at $0.36 per share. At current prices, that’s a robust yield near 6%, which should keep income-oriented investors interested.

Final Thoughts

Union Bankshares delivered a very clean quarter with solid profit growth and expanding book value. However, given the modest organic loan growth and the fact that the stock still commands a premium over its $17.46 book value, we maintain our Neutral/Hold stance. We aren't seeing signs of an economic slowdown here, but we also aren't seeing the aggressive growth that would lead us to a more bullish rating. For now, UNB is a steady, good yielding hold while we watch for more dynamic lending activity in the summer months.

data.title: VUSB: An Attractive Alternative To Money-Market Funds
data.description:
Over the last few years we have discussed how clients were inquiring about the best ways to redeploy cash holdings from non-interest-bearing (or low-interest-bearing) accounts at banks, credit unions, and even brokerages into higher-yielding fixed-income securities/funds (here is an article from September 2024 and another from April 2023). We have constantly made the argument that as long as rates were inverted, investors should focus on placing cash in money-market funds or similar-type investments, and as long as one is paid to sit in cash and short-term investments, it is beneficial to avoid duration and spread risk.

With the repositioning of the yield curve over the last few months - it is no longer inverted and has had some steepening—and the recent spike in rates with new risks being introduced (geopolitical and inflation fears), we think now is the time to redeploy some of the cash-like investments investors have into both the general market and looking at longer-maturity fixed-income ETFs.

What Are We Looking At?

As it pertains to adding risk in the fixed-income space, we think that the Vanguard Ultra-Short Bond ETF (VUSB) might be a logical replacement for some investors' cash-like holdings as it marginally adds to the risk profile (via credit and duration risk) but increases income to a yield of roughly 4.35%. The increase in yield is due to the added corporate exposure compared to most money-market funds (which are usually invested in government securities in order to transact at stable NAV/share values) and a longer average effective maturity.

An Overview Of The ETF

The Vanguard Ultra Short Bond ETF delivers a diversified fixed-income portfolio for investors that will generally offer a higher yield than money-market funds; however, the trade-off for investors is that unlike money-market funds, the ETF exposes investors to principal risk (meaning the investment is not pegged to $1). So, rising rates will generally lead to the holdings decreasing in price, while falling rates will see holdings increase in value.

While the possibility for changes in NAV, and thus unit price, of the ETF sounds scary, we believe the following chart shows that this ETF is pretty stable - especially when considering the extreme rate volatility we have seen in the market over the last two months. In short, the NAV has been within a $0.40 range, or less than 1% over a very volatile period for rates.

Based on the latest data from Vanguard, the ETF has seen its yield-to-maturity, or YTM, increase roughly 10% to 4.5% as of the last report date of 3/31/2026. Investors also moved about $200 million into the fund from the prior month-end, which, along with reinvestment, helped push up the average coupon to 4.20% as of 3/31/2026. We suspect that with rates having remained elevated, when Vanguard reports the numbers for the current month-end, the average coupon could possibly be higher based on the holdings rolling off via maturity in April and the new issue market (both investment-grade corporate and asset-backed) offering attractive coupons.

The following graph shows the weighted maturity of the underlying portfolio and looks much like we would expect, with a large "cash-like" buffer in the 0-12 month space and the largest maturity exposure falling in the 1-2 year space. The 2-3 year maturity exposure is a decent anchor. And, if buying smartly, this is what can help individual investors lock in a higher rate over money markets in falling rate environments.

With the current maturity profile of the Vanguard Ultra Short Bond ETF, as well as where yields currently are, we believe that investors could benefit from additional income (potentially 5-10% more per month) and some capital gains should yields head lower on positive geopolitical news. The potential increase in income would depend on how long we stay at current rates and the exact securities purchased for the ETF portfolio, but based off of the new deals coming to the investment-grade fixed-income market and what the portfolio tends to invest in, we think that another 2-3 months at current levels could certainly deliver that extra income, especially if we see continued buying in the 2-3 year maturity range (which would indicate asset-backed security and corporate bond buying).

Distributions

This ETF in particular has done a decent job of keeping distributions elevated, even with the yield curve normalizing and cash-like instruments no longer being the highest yielding in the market. Vanguard has done a nice job of gradually extending duration on this ETF in a well-thought-out way that appears to have helped drive income generation while avoiding pitfalls that can sometimes hit fixed-income investors. For instance, we like the fact that YTM is relatively in line with the average coupon, which indicates to us that the fund is smartly taking some positions focused solely on yield generation but overall trying to keep volatility in check by keeping those two numbers in line (if YTM was 4.50%, for instance, but the average coupon was 2.50%, then the portfolio would have much more volatility, especially if those lower coupon bonds had longer maturities).

Our Thoughts

While there are products out there that have stable NAV pricing or invest in floating-rate securities, which may provide more stable pricing than the Vanguard Ultra Short Bond ETF, we think the market is offering investors an opportunity to move some of their money-market exposure further out the yield curve to pick up additional income. If rates stay stable from here, it will take a while for money-market fund rates to catch up, and if rates decline from here, the money-market funds will see yields and distributions fall faster - which would both be wins for those making the trade.

If rates moved higher from these elevated levels, money market funds would see their yields and distributions increase much quicker; however, the real question is how much higher rates will go on the short end of the curve (and how quick that move will be). If, like us, you believe that rates are more likely to decline than increase and that no move (in either direction) will be fast, then the Vanguard Ultra Short Bond ETF seems like a good place to allocate some of your portfolio's short-term fixed-income exposure to increase monthly income.

data.title: A Supercharged AI Mega Force
data.description:
Transcript

Since we last took stock of the AI mega force in our 2026 Global Outlook, several shifts have increased our conviction that it’s even more powerful now. And some of those shifts give the U.S. a competitive edge on AI.

1) AI spending accelerates

Spending for the AI buildout - already historic - is accelerating even faster than expected, especially for the "hyperscalers" or mega-cap tech companies leading the buildout. Just since October, their capital expenditure estimates out to 2030 are up 25%.

2) Energy and political constraints

In our outlook, we laid out three constraining forces on the buildout: physical, political and financial.

Firstly, physical. The spike in gas prices since the start of the Middle East conflict enhances the U.S. and China’s AI edge. Data centers in Europe and other parts of Asia rely on grids powered by Middle East gas. China relies on such grids only minimally, and the U.S. is a net energy exporter.

3) Financing and market implications

The back-loaded revenue gains from the massive AI spend have seen companies take on more debt. Funding from private markets has also played a role. Some major IPOs for companies like OpenAI and Anthropic (ANTHRO) could test appetite in equity markets. But bond issuance from "hyperscaler" tech companies has been well-absorbed. That depth of capital markets in the U.S. is another advantage in the AI theme.

We stay overweight to U.S. and EM stocks after last week’s upgrade. We see larger-than-expected investment supercharging the AI mega force and focus on its beneficiaries.

______________

U.S.-Iran talks to end the Middle East conflict have boosted stocks to new record highs. Tech has helped drive the gains - and what’s becoming clearer is the building strength of the AI theme. Our conviction in the AI transformation grows as we see broad gains in revenues and already historic capital spending even as U.S. tech valuations are back on par with the overall market. This reinforces our U.S. equity overweight and preference for AI thematic opportunities.

Even bigger spending

Note: Bars show the evolution of hyperscaler capex estimates over the last six months. Hyperscalers include Google, Meta, Microsoft, Amazon and Oracle.

Equity markets have rebounded on hopes for a resolution to the Middle East conflict, with the S&P 500 hitting a new record high. Tech stocks and the AI theme have led the gains, with the U.S. tech sector up 11% so far this month. We are seeing broad improvements in the drivers of the AI theme across capital spending and revenues after a notable drop in valuations. First, we had already expected the fastest capital spending buildout in history as we laid out in our 2026 Global Outlook - and consensus expectations have only risen since then, with “hyperscaler” mega-cap tech companies’ estimates for 2026 to 2030 up over 25% since October. See the chart above. Second, we are seeing rapid revenue gains for some AI model builders. And third, U.S. tech valuations are now in line with the broader S&P 500 even as tech earnings expectations have risen sharply, as we noted last week.

In our 2026 Outlook, we identified three potential constraints on the AI buildout: power, politics and financing. The energy constraint is front and center and is starting to bind - but we have seen some improvements. “Behind the meter” power generation solutions - which generate power without a grid connection, help avoid potential delays from connecting to the grid and can limit impacts on local electricity prices - are set to move from being a marginal source to powering about 25-30% of new U.S. data centers in coming years, our compilation of equipment company estimates and Sightline Climate Data show. Yet, supply disruptions from the Middle East conflict have caused a sharp spike in natural gas prices - a key source for powering AI data centers. The impact is regional in nature: Europe and larger Asian economies feel the disruptions more, while the U.S. is a net exporter with much lower prices. We think this, combined with “behind the meter” solutions, gives the U.S. an edge on AI. China’s diversified energy mix and nuclear and renewable energy leadership means it is also less affected.

Watching political constraints

On the U.S. political front, anti-AI data center sentiment is rising and creating pressures that could constrain the AI buildout. Maine’s legislature passed a temporary ban on large new data centers - a first among states in a new trend. At least 12 other states are looking to pause further AI data center building. We are monitoring the impact of these potential restrictions and their scale heading into U.S. elections later this year.

The AI buildout financing has played out as we explained in our Outlook’s leveraging up theme. Demand for bonds from hyperscalers has been strong, with an Amazon sale seeing demand quadruple the total amount sold. We view this leverage as necessary to get over the hump between front-loaded investment and backloaded revenues - and think it’s healthy so far. Upcoming new listings of key AI model builders and players will test this financing in equity markets. The rapid revenue gains of some model builders should be supportive, such as Claude model-maker Anthropic reporting a tripling of revenue to over $30 billion since the end of 2025. The depth of U.S. capital markets is another edge in the AI transformation.

Our bottom line

We stay overweight to U.S. and EM stocks, and see the AI mega force accelerating. Recent developments, including energy disruptions, reinforce the U.S. edge in AI. We also like AI thematic opportunities in power and infrastructure.

This post originally appeared on BlackRock

data.title: The Real Price Of Oil Is Not What You Think
data.description:
In less than 48 hours the ceasefire will expire, and the U.S.–Iran war might enter a new phase of escalation. The U.S. wants to start a new round of negotiations today, but Iran doesn’t seem willing to accept. According to Iranian Foreign Ministry spokesman Esmaeil Baghaei, the U.S. “violated the ceasefire from the beginning of its implementation,” starting with the U.S. blockade of Iranian ports and closing with the overnight capture of the Iranian container ship. On the other hand, President Trump has also claimed that Iran violated the ceasefire by firing bullets at French and British ships in the Strait of Hormuz. The latter remains closed/highly restricted 52 days after the start of the war.

The Tuesday evening deadline is approaching, and both parties seem more distant than a couple of days ago. As a result, at the time of writing, crude oil WTI (CL1:COM) / Brent (CO1:COM) prices are up +6% while main U.S. stock market futures are down ~ 0.50%.

Why Should You Pay Attention To Physical Oil Prices?

With the current 6% increase, the Brent Oil price is ~ $95 per barrel, which is pretty high but at least not in triple digits. However, oil prices can vary greatly for multiple reasons, from the location in which it is extracted to the type of contract we are considering. The oil prices you see on popular websites like Seeking Alpha or TradingView are primarily Continuous Spot CFDs, basically future contracts whose expiry is the closest possible. These are the prices that set the baseline for oil prices and are used by traders who want to speculate on price fluctuations. These transactions are cash-settled, which means that you are speculating on the oil prices, but you are not actually buying the underlying barrels of oil.

On the other hand, if you are a refinery, you need the underlying barrels of oil for your operation, not a cash-settled transaction. However, the price you are going to pay is not the same, as you need to add the shipping & physical surcharges.

Typically, the physical oil (red line) tends to be a little higher than the paper oil (green line), but the closure of the Strait of Hormuz has created an unprecedented breakdown in the global energy supply chain.

While the whole market is focused on the paper oil (~$95 per barrel), the real threat for the economy lies in the physical oil (~$112 per barrel). This is the actual price that refineries are paying to run their operations (and it will probably be passed on to consumers), and despite the sharp drop, it is still 80% higher than the beginning of January. At the end of March, the physical oil price matched the 2022 highs, and it currently shows a massive $17 premium over the paper oil. What does this mean?

It means that companies that need physical oil don’t follow the same logic as those who just trade oil for speculative/hedging reasons. They are willing to pay a massive premium to keep running their activity, so it is a constant competition against peers for every single drop of oil. Traders don’t need the physical delivery, and they simply speculate on their expectations. Since the war is expected to end anytime soon, this explains why paper oil prices are much lower than physical oil.

However, I believe that between the two, physical oil is what investors should prioritize. Physical oil is a better indicator of the current state of the energy crisis, and $112 per barrel of oil is way too high to not expect a significant upward pressure in the global inflation rate over the next months. Four days ago, the Eurostat issued the inflation report for the month of March, and it doesn’t bode well.

The euro area annual inflation rate was 2.60% in March, way higher than the 1.90% of February. Needless to say, the most volatile component was energy, from -0.30% pp in February to +0.48% pp in March. We are approaching the end of April, and the physical oil price remains in triple digits, so I have lots of doubts that ECB headline inflation expectations are going to be met. The ECB staff has forecasted a 2.60% inflation rate in 2026; therefore, even though we are in the middle of an energy crisis, it shouldn’t increase from now on.

In my opinion, the EU won’t escape a higher inflation rate, and of course, the same reasoning applies to all the other major economies, including the U.S. Even though the market is pricing in lower physical oil prices in the second part of the year, both the July delivery (blue line) and December delivery (orange line) are above ~ $95 per barrel, ~ 60% higher than four months ago.

The energy supply shock is bound to haunt us for the entire 2026 (even if the war ends today), and it might even extend to the beginning of 2027 as well. Historically, energy crises don’t end in less than 2 months.

The Duration Of The Conflict Is The Key

The major negotiating leverage at Iran’s disposal is the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, so it is reasonable to believe that it will remain closed as long as a final agreement is reached. Besides this, there isn’t much that Iran can do to really harm the U.S. and the world economy. However, even though weaker from a military perspective, Iran has a significant advantage: it doesn’t need to rush to reach an agreement. After 47 years of disputes, reaching an agreement over the next months would be considered a success. But this kind of timing is definitely not good for the U.S. Imagine a scenario in which the Strait of Hormuz remains closed even during the summer session: physical oil prices would challenge previous highs, if not more.

Early last week, we had multiple pieces of positive news that made us believe that the war was almost over, especially the Israel–Lebanon 10-day ceasefire. Furthermore, a new round of negotiations between the U.S. and Iran was expected before the ceasefire deadline (Tuesday evening) in Islamabad, avoiding a new phase of escalation. However, things changed pretty fast over the weekend: Israel didn’t stop attacking Lebanon despite the ceasefire, and Iran, contrary to the U.S., stated that it has no plans to send negotiators to Islamabad. Why? According to a Foreign Ministry spokesman, the U.S. has violated the ceasefire multiple times, and it is ready to strike back at Israel and the U.S. if they start new attacks. Of course, President Trump is not appreciating at all the Iranian unwillingness to find an agreement:

We’re offering a very fair and reasonable DEAL, and I hope they take it because, if they don’t, the United States is going to knock out every single Power Plant, and every single Bridge, in Iran. NO MORE MR. NICE GUY! They’ll come down fast, they’ll come down easy and, if they don’t take the DEAL, it will be my Honor to do what has to be done, which should have been done to Iran, by other Presidents, for the last 47 years. IT’S TIME FOR THE IRAN KILLING MACHINE TO END!

President DONALD J. TRUMP.

Basically, 52 days have passed since the start of the war, and we are back to square one. The U.S. and Iran still have very different opinions, but what investors care about the most is that the Strait of Hormuz is still closed. The longer it stays closed, the more likely the stagflation scenario gets. As of now, the stock market is totally downplaying the likelihood of a stagflationary period; in fact, it has recently set a new all-time high.

From a purely technical perspective, the S&P 500 Index (SP500) is close to beating a long-term trend started during the pandemic. If it gets above the resistance, we are directed toward uncharted territory; if it goes down, the worst-case seems to be 6,000 for now. To get to this point, I believe the war should restart on Wednesday, or at least, the economy should experience higher-than-expected inflation rates over the next weeks or a weak earnings season. With such high energy prices, investors have a lot of questions on the ROI of the billions of dollars that AI hyperscalers are going to invest.

data.title: Moog: This Quiet Defense Winner Still Has Upside
data.description:
Moog has gained 6.9% since my last report outperforming the S&P 500’s 3% gain. That is not a huge outperformance but still more than twice the index return. I believe that is actually quite remarkable given that aerospace and defense stocks have not been favored by the market since the war in Iran started. In this report, I discuss the opportunities that Moog has, the upcoming earnings expectations and present my increased price target supporting my buy rating.

Moog Could Have Elevated Opportunities Amid Rising Tensions

What we have seen is increased demand for missile and missile defense. While contracts may be awarded, I don’t view increased missile defense as a substantial near-term driver. The reason for that is that defense prime contractors are already executing on previously established frameworks to increase production and additional demand would more often than not be either for even higher production in years from now or extending peak production levels. So, near-term opportunities may be limited. There may be some funds flowing into the supply chain to start expanding the capacity beyond previously established levels to support replenishing the missile stockpiles, but I do not expect those to be big dollars at this stage.

Moog supplies motion control, actuation and control systems that are used in missiles for guidance and propulsion control and fighter jets such as the F-35 for flight controls. The company’s products are also used in turrets and satellite systems. With the eye on the war in Iran, the drivers would be replenishment demand for missiles and higher maintenance and spare parts for air operations. Moog is a supplier to the defense primes and with a potentially prolonged conflict, we could see some sort of supply chain lock in which could give Moog good backlog and revenue visibility and strong pricing power as the war environment pivots price sensitivity to delivery-over-cost. Since Moog provides products that cannot be easily replaced by other suppliers, it has superior pricing power without bidding risks since it is simply part of an integrated supply chain of the defense primes. In case of a ground war, Moog could even see stronger demand from ground systems and additional air operations demanding more maintenance and spares.

Part of all this is, however, offset by potential risks of contract costs inflating and that is also why aerospace and defense stocks declined when the war in Iran started.

Moog Expected To Show Double Digit Sales Growth With EPS Growth In The Twenties

Moog will be reporting its Q2 2025 earnings on April 24. Wall Street analysts are expecting the company to report 10.5% year-on-year growth likely driven by strength in commercial aviation, defense and space markets. Over the past month, the estimates increased by 1.5% and by 0.5% and 2.9% over the past three and six months.

EPS estimates point to an even stronger increase with 23.1% growth to $2.36 with no change over the past month and upward revisions of 2.6% and 2.75% over the past six and three months. What will be interesting is any comment from management on tariffs. The Supreme Court of the United States ruled that tariffs imposed under IEEPA were illegal, but the administration replaced those with new tariffs using different parts of the law and tariffs under Section 301 and Section 232 remain.

Moog Remains Attractive As Growth Drivers Better Align

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For Moog, the expectations have not substantially changed with a 0.6% downward revision on EBITDA and free cash flow for 2026-2028. I have been covering Moog since May 2023 and since then the stock has returned over 200% showing a roughly 3:1 performance against the S&P 500. Moog’s problem back then was that it had cost overruns at some of its space programs and the company had a 2.4x net debt leverage. While that was not awful, there was a need for Moog to increase its free cash flows to better carry debt maturities. For FY26, the expectation is that the company would generate nearly $200 million in free cash flow compared to a $37 million free cash flow burn in FY23. The company has substantially improved and while they still need to refinance some debt as they did earlier this year to streamline the debt maturities, their ability to repay debt from free cash flow is improving in the years ahead and the net debt leverage has improved substantially, which also enables share repurchases.

The company is expected to grow revenues by 8% annually and I am currently expecting EBITDA growth slightly below that driven by some delays in commercial aerospace production ramp ups and lower margins in the early phase of new military programs, but the free cash flow is expected to grow by 42% annually.

To value Moog, I am using the average of the company’s median EV/EBITDA implied price target of $227.52 and the peer group valuation of $505. This brings the price target to $366.26 against my prior price target of $349 and indicates 13.5% upside. One thing that we need to keep in mind is the current volatility in aerospace and defense stocks, which may provide some near-term noise in the share price performance.

For Moog, I am expecting margins of around 15% with clean free cash flow growth as capital expenditures are expected to be around $150-$160 million. With the improving cash flows that should lead to capital intensity tapering to 30% from 127% of operating cash flow years ago. Even with returning all free cash flow to shareholders, we see that the company’s net debt leverage is set to drop to 1.5x by FY28 and heading into the final years of this decade and into the next, the company should be able to see more robust growth from commercial airplane programs running at full capacity all at the same time.

Risk On Israel Shipments For Moog

One risk for Moog are the shipments to Israel. Moog's UK subsidiary sent a shipment bound for Israel through Belgium and those shipments have been seized due to missing declarations. Moog is providing actuators for airplanes used to train F-35 pilots for Israel as well as actuators for fire control systems and spare parts. The problem seems to be that Moog changed its shipping from FedEx (FDX) to UPS (UPS) and the carrier should normally apply for a transit license. So, this potentially could be a risk for Moog and/or UPS.

Conclusion: Moog Looks Overvalued But I Believe It is Not

Moog may look overvalued when we consider the company’s median EV/EBITDA. However, we note that the company has been working its way through a very challenging operational performance and debt load and it has been doing so rather well and that should provide support for the company to trade more in line with peers eventually. As a result, I am maintaining my buy rating for Moog.
